title: Climate Refugees: A Growing Crisis
summary: A narrator examines how climate change is forcing millions of people to abandon their homes and the inadequate legal frameworks designed to protect them.
body: The term 'climate refugee' does not yet appear in international law, and that gap is becoming increasingly difficult to ignore. Under the 1951 Refugee Convention, a person qualifies for protection only if they face persecution based on race, religion, nationality, political opinion, or membership of a particular social group. Displacement caused by floods, droughts, or rising sea levels does not fit any of these categories, leaving millions of people without formal legal status when they are forced to move. Conservative estimates suggest that by 2050, up to two hundred million people could be displaced by climate-related causes. That figure rivals the total population of several large nations combined, yet the international community has been slow to craft a coherent response. Some climate scientists argue that the term 'refugee' itself may be misleading, because many climate-displaced people do not cross international borders at all. They move from coastal zones to inland cities, from drought-stricken farmland to overcrowded urban centres, often within the same country. This internal displacement is harder to count, harder to govern, and harder to reverse. The Pacific Island nation of Tuvalu has become a symbol of the crisis. Its highest point sits just four metres above sea level, and saltwater intrusion has already rendered large areas of arable land unproductive. The government has negotiated land agreements with New Zealand, effectively planning for a future in which the entire population is resettled abroad. This is not migration in any traditional sense; it is the state-managed dissolution of a homeland. Wealthier nations are not immune. Parts of coastal Louisiana have been permanently abandoned as the Gulf of Mexico encroaches on land that was inhabited for generations. Villages in Alaska are sinking as permafrost melts beneath them. In Bangladesh, an estimated seventeen percent of the country's land area could be submerged by mid-century, displacing around eighteen million people. The asymmetry is striking: the communities least responsible for historical carbon emissions are bearing the greatest costs. Closing the legal gap is technically possible. Several proposals have been put forward at the United Nations to amend the 1951 Convention or create a parallel instrument specifically for climate displacement. Each proposal has stalled, largely because wealthy countries fear that a new legal category would commit them to accepting large numbers of additional asylum seekers. The reluctance is understandable from a domestic political standpoint, but it leaves the most vulnerable populations without protection. In the meantime, civil society organisations are documenting cases, building case law, and pressing courts to recognise climate causation as a factor in deportation appeals. Slowly, the legal architecture is being shaped from the bottom up. Whether it moves fast enough to match the pace of displacement remains the central question of our era.

annotations.meaningVi: An international treaty that defines who is a refugee and what rights they are entitled to.
annotations.exampleEn: The 1951 Convention is the cornerstone of international refugee law.

annotations.meaningVi: Cruel or unfair treatment of a person, especially because of their race, religion, or political views.
annotations.exampleEn: She fled her country to escape political persecution.

annotations.meaningVi: The forced movement of people from their home area.
annotations.exampleEn: The flood caused widespread displacement of coastal communities.

annotations.meaningVi: A cautious calculation that deliberately avoids overstating a number.
annotations.exampleEn: Conservative estimates put the damage at ten billion dollars.

annotations.meaningVi: Movement of people within the borders of their own country.
annotations.exampleEn: Internal displacement is harder to track than cross-border movement.

annotations.meaningVi: The movement of saline ocean water into freshwater aquifers or soil, making land unfit for farming.
annotations.exampleEn: Saltwater intrusion has destroyed rice paddies near the coast.

annotations.meaningVi: Land suitable for growing crops.
annotations.exampleEn: Climate change is reducing the amount of arable land worldwide.

annotations.meaningVi: The breaking up or ending of something, such as a community or state.
annotations.exampleEn: The dissolution of the village was a tragic outcome of the flooding.

annotations.meaningVi: Ground that stays frozen throughout the year, found in polar and sub-polar regions.
annotations.exampleEn: Melting permafrost releases greenhouse gases into the atmosphere.

annotations.meaningVi: A lack of equality or balance between two things.
annotations.exampleEn: There is a stark asymmetry between who causes pollution and who suffers most.

annotations.meaningVi: Carbon dioxide and other gases released into the atmosphere, mainly by burning fossil fuels.
annotations.exampleEn: Industrialised nations have produced the majority of historical carbon emissions.

annotations.meaningVi: To make changes to a law, agreement, or document to improve or update it.
annotations.exampleEn: Several countries want to amend the convention to include climate causes.

annotations.meaningVi: A separate but related legal document created to address a gap in an existing one.
annotations.exampleEn: A parallel instrument could protect climate-displaced people without changing the original treaty.

annotations.meaningVi: Stopped making progress; stuck.
annotations.exampleEn: Negotiations on the new treaty have stalled for three years.

annotations.meaningVi: A person who has left their home country and is seeking legal protection in another country.
annotations.exampleEn: Governments are divided on how many asylum seekers to accept each year.

annotations.meaningVi: Non-governmental groups — charities, advocacy groups, community organisations — that operate between the individual and the state.
annotations.exampleEn: Civil society organisations played a key role in pressuring the government to act.

annotations.meaningVi: Law established by the outcome of past court decisions, rather than by legislation.
annotations.exampleEn: Activists are building case law that links climate change to human rights violations.

annotations.meaningVi: A legal challenge by a person against an order to remove them from a country.
annotations.exampleEn: Her lawyers cited climate conditions in the deportation appeal.

annotations.meaningVi: To gradually move into or take over an area belonging to someone else.
annotations.exampleEn: The sea is encroaching on low-lying farmland every year.

annotations.meaningVi: The overall framework of laws and treaties that governs a particular area.
annotations.exampleEn: The legal architecture for refugee protection is over seventy years old.

annotations.meaningVi: A clear, logical, and well-organised reaction to a problem.
annotations.exampleEn: The world has yet to develop a coherent response to climate displacement.

grammarNotes.title: Future perfect and modal speculation about the future
grammarNotes.bodyVi: The passage uses modal constructions to express varying degrees of certainty about future events. 'Up to two hundred million people could be displaced by 2050' uses 'could + passive infinitive' to indicate possibility. 'An estimated seventeen percent… could be submerged by mid-century' repeats this pattern. In formal academic writing, 'could', 'may', and 'might' signal that figures are projections rather than certainties, which is important for intellectual honesty when discussing contested scientific data. Examples: Up to two hundred million people could be displaced by climate-related causes. / An estimated seventeen percent of the country's land area could be submerged by mid-century.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses modal constructions to express varying degrees of certainty about future events. 'Up to two hundred million people could be displaced by 2050' uses 'could + passive infinitive' to indicate possibility. 'An estimated seventeen percent… could be submerged by mid-century' repeats this pattern. In formal academic writing, 'could', 'may', and 'might' signal that figures are projections rather than certainties, which is important for intellectual honesty when discussing contested scientific data. Examples: Up to two hundred million people could be displaced by climate-related causes. / An estimated seventeen percent of the country's land area could be submerged by mid-century.

grammarNotes.title: Passive voice for objectivity and emphasis
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Academic and journalistic writing favours the passive voice when the agent (the doer of the action) is unknown, unimportant, or deliberately omitted to keep focus on what happened rather than who did it. 'Several proposals have been put forward' omits the specific proposers; 'the legal architecture is being shaped from the bottom up' emphasises the process rather than any single actor. The passive also allows the writer to front-load the topic of interest (proposals, architecture) rather than the actor. Examples: Several proposals have been put forward at the United Nations. / Slowly, the legal architecture is being shaped from the bottom up.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Academic and journalistic writing favours the passive voice when the agent (the doer of the action) is unknown, unimportant, or deliberately omitted to keep focus on what happened rather than who did it. 'Several proposals have been put forward' omits the specific proposers; 'the legal architecture is being shaped from the bottom up' emphasises the process rather than any single actor. The passive also allows the writer to front-load the topic of interest (proposals, architecture) rather than the actor. Examples: Several proposals have been put forward at the United Nations. / Slowly, the legal architecture is being shaped from the bottom up.

questions.prompt: Why does the 1951 Refugee Convention not protect climate-displaced people?
options: It only covers people displaced within their own country
It requires persecution based on specific identity grounds not met by climate displacement
It has been replaced by a newer treaty
It only applies to displacement caused by war
questions.explanation: The convention requires persecution based on race, religion, nationality, political opinion, or social group — causes that exclude climate displacement.
questions.hint: Listen for what conditions are listed under the convention.

questions.prompt: What conservative estimate does the passage give for climate-displaced people by 2050?
questions.explanation: The passage states 'up to two hundred million people could be displaced by climate-related causes'.
questions.hint: Listen for the figure mentioned alongside the year 2050.

questions.prompt: Why do some scientists find the term 'refugee' misleading for climate-displaced people?
options: Because many do not cross international borders
Because they can always return home
Because the term is legally protected
questions.explanation: The passage says 'many climate-displaced people do not cross international borders at all'.
questions.hint: Listen for the argument scientists make about borders.

questions.prompt: Which characteristic makes internal displacement particularly challenging?
options: It is illegal under most constitutions
It is harder to count, govern, and reverse
It always leads to cross-border migration eventually
It is concentrated only in island nations
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'this internal displacement is harder to count, harder to govern, and harder to reverse'.
questions.hint: Listen for the three adjectives used to describe internal displacement.

questions.prompt: What is the highest elevation of Tuvalu above sea level?
questions.explanation: The passage states Tuvalu's 'highest point sits just four metres above sea level'.
questions.hint: Listen for the specific measurement given about Tuvalu.

questions.prompt: What arrangement has Tuvalu made in anticipation of full submersion?
options: It has requested UN protectorate status
It has negotiated land agreements with New Zealand
It has purchased territory in Australia
It has applied to merge with Fiji
questions.explanation: The passage says 'the government has negotiated land agreements with New Zealand'.
questions.hint: Listen for which country Tuvalu has an arrangement with.

questions.prompt: How does the narrator describe Tuvalu's situation in contrast to ordinary migration?
options: As state-managed dissolution of a homeland
questions.explanation: The narrator calls it 'the state-managed dissolution of a homeland'.
questions.hint: Listen for the phrase that contrasts with 'traditional migration'.

questions.prompt: Which part of the United States is mentioned as already experiencing permanent abandonment of land?
questions.explanation: The passage refers to 'parts of coastal Louisiana' being permanently abandoned.
questions.hint: Listen for the US state mentioned in relation to the Gulf of Mexico.

questions.prompt: What climate phenomenon is causing Alaskan villages to sink?
questions.explanation: The passage states 'villages in Alaska are sinking as permafrost melts beneath them'.
questions.hint: Listen for the specific geological process in Alaska.

questions.prompt: What percentage of Bangladesh's land could be submerged by mid-century?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'an estimated seventeen percent of the country's land area could be submerged'.
questions.hint: Listen for the percentage figure related to Bangladesh.

questions.prompt: How many people in Bangladesh could be displaced by mid-century?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'displacing around eighteen million people'.
questions.hint: Listen for the number of people mentioned alongside Bangladesh.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator mean by 'asymmetry' in the context of climate change?
options: Wealthier countries suffer more than poorer ones
The countries least responsible for emissions bear the greatest costs
Island nations emit more carbon than mainland countries
Climate change affects only coastal regions
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'communities least responsible for historical carbon emissions are bearing the greatest costs'.
questions.hint: Listen for how the narrator explains the asymmetry.

questions.prompt: Why have UN proposals to protect climate-displaced people stalled?
options: There is no scientific consensus on climate change
Wealthy countries fear a legal obligation to accept more asylum seekers
questions.explanation: The passage states wealthy countries fear the category 'would commit them to accepting large numbers of additional asylum seekers'.
questions.hint: Listen for the reason wealthy countries have resisted proposals.

questions.prompt: How does the narrator characterise wealthy countries' reluctance to act?
options: As morally inexcusable and politically dishonest
As understandable politically but leaving vulnerable people unprotected
As a temporary position likely to change soon
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'the reluctance is understandable from a domestic political standpoint, but it leaves the most vulnerable populations without protection'.
questions.hint: Listen for the balanced assessment the narrator gives.

questions.prompt: What are civil society organisations doing to address the legal gap?
options: Lobbying governments to increase foreign aid
Documenting cases, building case law, and pressing courts on deportation appeals
Organising climate summits at the United Nations
Funding the relocation of Pacific Island populations
questions.explanation: The narrator describes civil society 'documenting cases, building case law, and pressing courts to recognise climate causation in deportation appeals'.
questions.hint: Listen for the three activities civil society organisations are engaged in.

questions.prompt: What does the phrase 'shaped from the bottom up' mean in this passage?
options: The law is being rewritten by government ministries
Legal frameworks are emerging through grassroots action rather than top-level treaties
New buildings are being constructed in flood zones
Climate data is gathered from the ocean floor
questions.explanation: The context refers to civil society groups — not governments — slowly building new legal precedents.
questions.hint: Think about who is doing the shaping according to the final paragraph.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator identify as 'the central question of our era'?
options: Whether wealthier nations will reduce carbon emissions
Whether legal change will happen fast enough to keep pace with displacement
Whether Tuvalu can be saved from submersion
Whether the 1951 Convention should be abolished
questions.explanation: The final sentence asks 'whether it moves fast enough to match the pace of displacement'.
questions.hint: Listen to the very last sentence of the passage.

cloze.template: The 1951 Refugee {{b1}} does not cover people displaced by climate events because it requires {{b2}} based on identity grounds such as race or religion. Conservative estimates suggest up to two hundred million people could be {{b3}} by 2050. Many climate-displaced people do not cross {{b4}} borders, making their movement a form of {{b5}} displacement. Tuvalu, whose highest point is just four metres above {{b6}} level, has negotiated {{b7}} agreements with New Zealand as it faces complete submersion. This represents the {{b8}}-managed dissolution of a homeland. In the United States, parts of coastal {{b9}} have been permanently abandoned. In Alaska, villages are sinking because {{b10}} is melting beneath them. Bangladesh could lose seventeen percent of its {{b11}} area, displacing around eighteen million people. The {{b12}} is stark: those least responsible for carbon {{b13}} suffer the most. UN proposals to amend the convention have {{b14}} because wealthy nations fear new {{b15}} obligations. Civil society organisations are building {{b16}} law through court cases, slowly shaping the legal {{b17}} from the bottom up.

blanks.explanation: The 1951 international treaty on refugee protection.
Cruel treatment based on identity characteristics.
The type of borders that refugees traditionally cross.
The reference point for measuring coastal elevation.
The type of agreement Tuvalu made with New Zealand.
The US coastal state mentioned alongside the Gulf of Mexico.
Permanently frozen ground found in polar regions.
The percentage refers to this type of area in Bangladesh.
The imbalance between who causes harm and who suffers.
Carbon gases released into the atmosphere.
The type of obligations that frightens wealthy countries.
The overall framework of laws and treaties.

translationVi: Bài nghe phân tích lý do tại sao những người bị buộc phải rời bỏ nhà cửa vì biến đổi khí hậu không được bảo vệ theo Công ước Tị nạn năm 1951. Hàng trăm triệu người có thể bị di dời vào năm 2050, trong đó nhiều người không vượt biên giới quốc gia, khiến họ không được tính là người tị nạn theo định nghĩa pháp lý. Từ Tuvalu đến Louisiana, những cộng đồng ít gây ô nhiễm nhất lại gánh chịu hậu quả nặng nề nhất. Các đề xuất của Liên Hợp Quốc nhằm lấp đầy khoảng trống pháp lý này đã bị đình trệ, nhưng các tổ chức xã hội dân sự đang dần xây dựng hệ thống pháp luật từ dưới lên thông qua các vụ kiện tòa án.
criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator says wealthy countries' reluctance to reform refugee law is 'understandable from a domestic political standpoint'. Do you agree? To what extent should domestic political concerns override the humanitarian needs of climate-displaced people, and who should bear the cost of climate displacement?
